# Image Description

This sepia-toned photograph shows a horse-drawn delivery wagon parked on a street in front of a storefront. A white or light-colored horse is hitched to a wooden wagon with large spoked wheels. Two people are visible on the wagon: a man in a white shirt and cap standing on an elevated platform at the back, and a woman seated in the middle section of the wagon. The wagon carries various product boxes and containers, including visible text reading "Milk," "Orange Ade," and what appears to be "Saratoga." The storefront behind the wagon displays signage that reads "Bottlers Supplies" and "Real Flavor & Extract" across its awning. Additional storefronts and windows are visible in the background. The photograph appears to be from the early 20th century, documenting a commercial milk or beverage delivery business. The image shows wear consistent with its age, with visible creases and discoloration throughout.
Collection name: Jesse Brown Cook Scrapbooks Documenting San Francisco History and Law Enforcement

# Stereoscopic Card Description

This is a stereoscopic card (stereoview) numbered 58, produced by Keystone View Company as part of their "Manufacturers and Comforts" educational series. The card contains two nearly identical sepia-toned photographs placed side-by-side, designed to create a three-dimensional effect when viewed through a stereoscope.

The images depict a woman in early 20th-century clothing (long dark dress, white apron, and hat) hand-feeding ice cream to a small black bear cub in what appears to be an outdoor enclosure. The bear stands on its hind legs while the woman holds out a spoon or implement containing the ice cream. Behind them is a brick structure with metal mesh fencing. Vegetation and trees are visible in the background. The caption at the bottom reads: "21084 Mrs. Herbert Hoover Feeds Ice Cream to a Small Black Bear Cub, Seward, Alaska." Text along the left margin indicates "Keystone View Company, Manufacturers Comforts Publishers," while text on the right margin shows publication information typical of Keystone View products.

# Street Scene Description

This black and white photograph shows a Parisian street scene with a large group of people gathered on a stone-paved courtyard. The central focus is approximately twenty men, mostly wearing long coats and hats typical of early-to-mid twentieth century fashion, assembled in a cluster in the middle of the square. On the left side stands a single man in a lighter colored coat, and additional figures are visible in the background and right side of the frame.

The background features ornate Haussmann-style Parisian architecture consisting of multi-story stone buildings with mansard roofs. The most prominent building on the left displays large arched windows, decorative stone carvings beneath the second-floor balcony, wrought iron railings, and shuttered windows. A sign reading "COIFFEUR" (barber/hairdresser) is visible on the building's facade. Additional buildings to the right show similar period architecture with regular window patterns and chimneys.

The ground surface is composed of large rectangular stone paving blocks arranged in a regular pattern. The cobblestone courtyard appears to be a public square or courtyard area, likely in Paris. The architectural style and clothing suggest this photograph dates from the 1940s-1950s era.

# Image Description

This vintage promotional card showcases Spreckels Rotisserie, a San Francisco establishment from the early 20th century. At the top, an ornate gold and brown banner bears the restaurant's name and indicates it was located on the 15th floor of the Call Building, operated by A. Wolff. The card's publisher is credited as Edward H. Mitchell of San Francisco in the top right corner.

The main illustration depicts two elegantly dressed diners—a man in a dark suit and a woman in a coral-colored dress—seated at a white-clothed table on the restaurant's terrace. Between them sits a bottle, decorative centerpiece, and wine glass. A large window behind them frames an expansive cityscape view showing San Francisco's dense urban landscape with numerous buildings and hills. To the left side of the card, a separate image displays the iconic Call Building, a tall ornate tower with a distinctive domed top, labeled "CAFE."

At the bottom of the card, text reads "Ice Cream Parlor, Cafe and Restaurant" and notes the location is "250 Feet Above the Bay." Decorative potted plants flank the scene on either side.
Collection name: Walter Robert and Gail Lynn Kransky collection of Edward H. Mitchell postcards

# Image Description

This is a hand-colored postcard from the Los Angeles Pigeon Farm near Elysian Park, showing an expansive facility with numerous white pigeons covering the ground in the foreground. The large flock of birds fills most of the lower two-thirds of the image. Behind the pigeons are several wooden structures including long, low buildings with dark roofs and walls, along with fencing and what appears to be storage areas stacked with light-colored materials on the right side. A water channel or irrigation ditch runs along the left side of the facility. The background shows distant hills and a dense line of tall evergreen trees silhouetted against the sky. A wooden utility pole is visible on the right side. The red text at the top reads "Los Angeles Pigeon Farm, Near Elysian Park." The postcard shows signs of age with a cream-colored background and visible wear, featuring the characteristic coloring and printing style of early 20th-century postcards. A color reference scale is printed at the bottom of the image.
